First, I know that valproate is a medication commonly used for seizures, like in epilepsy.

One of the known side effects of valproate is a condition called "valproate-induced trigeminal neuralgia."
Trigeminal neuralgia is a type of facial pain, often described as a sharp, shooting pain along the distribution of one or more trigeminal nerves.
The trigeminal nerve has several divisions: ophthalmic, maxillary, and mandibular.
The lesion here is affecting the ophthalmic and maxillary divisions, which suggests it's related to the trigeminal nerve.
Since the lesion has been present since birth and hasn't changed, it's likely a congenital condition.
Valproate can cause demyelination in the trigeminal nerve, leading to this type of lesion.
This is sometimes referred to as "valproate-induced demyelination," which typically affects the trigeminal nerve, causing the characteristic plaques.
So putting it all together, the child is on valproate, has a lesion in the trigeminal nerve distribution, and the lesion is stable since birth.
This points towards valproate-induced trigeminal neuralgia or valproate-induced demyelination affecting the trigeminal nerve.

The child has a stable vascular lesion involving the ophthalmic and maxillary divisions of the trigeminal nerve, which is consistent with valproate-induced trigeminal neuralgia or valproate-induced demyelination.
